page: Amazon Web Services i4g.8xlarge is a storage-optimized server instance powered by the Nitro hypervisor and AWS Graviton processors. It features 32 dedicated physical cores on the arm64 architecture, utilizing the AWS Neoverse-N1 CPU family running at 2.5 GHz. The hardware configuration includes 256.0 GB of system memory, providing 8.0 GB of RAM per core, and is bundled with 7500 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. Network performance is supported by a baseline bandwidth of 19 Gbps.

Benchmark data indicates average multi-core CPU performance, with a stress-ng multi-core score of 48581.53 and a Geekbench multi-core score of 11678.0. Single-core performance is relatively weak, as shown by a Geekbench single-core score of 962.0 and a weak single-threaded gzip compression rate of 20.27 GB/sec. Memory bandwidth is solid, reaching 1893.65 GB/sec for cached small-block reads and 151.6 GB/sec for uncached large-block reads. Database operations perform in the average tier, with Redis SET operations achieving 36.96 million operations per second and Passmark database operations scoring 20435.28.

The instance presents a clear tradeoff between high-capacity local NVMe storage and single-threaded processing speed. While multi-threaded tasks like bzip3 decompression and software compilation perform at average levels, single-threaded text processing and small-model LLM inference speeds are weak. From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, the combination of 32 dedicated ARM cores, a 1:8 memory-to-core ratio, and 7.5 TB of bundled NVMe SSD storage offers a dense resource profile for storage-heavy workloads. This server is suited for database hosting, transactional workloads, static web serving, and multi-threaded data processing tasks.
description: Amazon Web Services i4g.8xlarge is an arm64 storage-optimized instance featuring 32 dedicated physical cores powered by AWS Graviton Neoverse-N1 processors running at 2.5 GHz. It is equipped with 256.0 GB of RAM and 7500 GB of local NVMe SSD storage, operating on the Nitro hypervisor with a 19 Gbps baseline network bandwidth. Benchmark results show average multi-core CPU performance, with a stress-ng multi-core score of 48581.53, alongside average database operations and memory bandwidth. However, single-threaded performance is weak, as evidenced by a Geekbench single-core score of 962.0 and weak text processing. This resource profile offers qualitative cost efficiency for storage-intensive deployments. It is suited for database operations, multi-threaded data processing, and static web serving workloads requiring high-capacity local NVMe storage.
